sales.title: LESBOS. Mytilene. Ca. 377-326 BC. EL sixth-stater or hecte (11mm, 2.52 gm, 11h). NGC VF 4/5 - 3/5. Head of Apollo Carneius right, with horn of Ammon / Eagle standing right, head reverted, wings closed; within linear frame, all within incuse square. Bodenstedt 104. Gulbenkian 886. Marigold backlight.   Carneia was a traditional tribal festival in Sparta and in the Peloponnese and Doric cities in Magna Grecia. It was a celebration of Carneus (from 'Karnos' or 'Karneios,' probably meaning ram), the god of the harvest, flocks, and herds. It is unclear whether Carneus is an emanation from Apollo or if he was an older Peloponnesian deity that was later displaced by Apollo. Carneia seems to have been a primarily agrarian festival but with military and reconciliatory elements as well.  It was celebrated through two parts. In the first, five unmarried youths would chase a man bedecked with garlands, securing fertility for next year's crops if they caught him. In the second part, nine tents were set up in the country in which nine citizens would feast in honor of the gods. They would also sacrifice an animal (possibly a human in earlier celebrations), representing the vegetation spirit. The cycle of death and rebirth in this ritual, representing the seasonal death and rebirth of plants and the harvest, is like many other vegetation dramas celebrated by ancient cultures such as Cerealia in Rome and the Eleusinian Mysteries in honor of Demeter.  The festival suspended all military operations during its observance, which lasted from the 7th to the 15th of the month Carneus (approximately August). The tradition of Carneia was so ingrained that it affected military movements, as seen in instances like the delay in Spartan assistance during the Battle of Marathon, and the dispatch of a smaller advance guard instead of the main Spartan force during the Battle of Thermopylae.
